metadata.title: Propose and Critique a Confounder Set with Claude
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration: Claude lists twelve plausible confounders for your study. Two of them are actually mediators, and one is a collider in disguise.
Most applied work conflates three distinct node types: true confounders, mediators on the causal path, and colliders affected by both treatment and outcome. The structural audit separates them.
We ask Claude to propose eight to ten potential confounders for a study of algorithm deployment on health-system efficiency, with signed edges and mechanistic justifications.

props.command:
claude "For study: effect of algorithm deployment
  on health-system efficiency (X→Y).
Propose 8 potential confounders with:
  signed edges (does each ↑ or ↓ both X and Y?)
  one-sentence mechanistic justification."

beats.narration: Claude returns ten candidates. Hospital size, IT infrastructure maturity, and patient complexity are true confounders — they affect both algorithm adoption and efficiency. But workflow standardization is a mediator: it is on the path algorithm causes standardization causes efficiency.

props.code:
# confounder_audit.md — Claude output

Candidate confounders:
1. hospital_size         → both ↑ (true confound)
2. IT_maturity           → both ↑ (true confound)
3. patient_complexity    → X↑ Y↓ (true confound)
4. workflow_standardize  → ← mediator (X→M→Y)
5. staff_resistance      → ← mediator (X→M→Y)
6. regulatory_pressure   → both ↑ (true confound)
7. admin_oversight       ← collider? (X→C←Y)
8. budget_allocation     → both ↑ (true confound)
9. EHR_quality           → both ↑ (true confound)
10.peer_adoption_rate    → both ↑ (true confound)

beats.narration: The structural audit: items four and five — workflow standardization and staff resistance — are mediators. They lie on the causal path from algorithm deployment to efficiency. Conditioning on them would block the path we are trying to estimate. Item seven — admin oversight — is a potential collider.
We ask Claude to separate its list into structural confounders — those with direct arrows into both X and Y from outside the causal path — versus proxy confounders that are downstream of X.

props.command:
claude "Reclassify your confounder list into:
  (1) structural confounders (external cause
      of both X and Y)
  (2) proxy confounders (downstream of X)
  (3) colliders (caused by both X and Y)"

beats.narration: After reclassification: five structural confounders, two mediators relabeled as proxy confounders, one collider. Claude correctly moves workflow standardization and staff resistance out of the adjustment set. Admin oversight is flagged as a collider — do not condition on it.

Proposing confounders is easy. The structural audit — distinguishing true confounders from mediators from colliders — is the work. Conditioning on a mediator blocks the causal path. Conditioning on a collider opens a spurious one.
Next: build a DoWhy causal effect estimate and see how much the adjusted estimate differs from the naive one.
Like and subscribe for more causal reasoning with Claude.
